planting.note: Direct sow fresh seeds in fall or cold-stratify 60–90 days; gum bumelia prefers well-drained to dry, slightly acidic to neutral or alkaline soil in full sun to light shade. A slow-growing, long-lived, tough native of dry, rocky, or sandy upland sites.

directions: Plant in well-drained to dry, slightly acidic to neutral or alkaline soil in full sun; gum bumelia is a thorny native small tree or large shrub of dry uplands, rocky hillsides, and dry woodlands across the south-central US from Kansas to Florida. The small, black, olive-like fruits are edible and mildly sweet — consumed by numerous birds including mockingbirds, thrushes, and cedar waxwings — making it an outstanding wildlife plant for dry, sunny, naturalistic landscapes. The thick, spiny branches provide secure nesting habitat for mockingbirds and other cavity-nesters and the dense crown offers excellent cover. The milky sap was historically chewed as gum by Indigenous peoples (hence 'gum elastic' and 'gum bumelia'). Extremely drought tolerant and long-lived. The thorny habit makes it effective as an impenetrable wildlife hedge or barrier plant.

planting.note: Direct sow fresh seeds in fall in moist to well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; buckthorn bumelia grows naturally in moist to mesic forests, forest edges, and bottomland woodlands across the Gulf and Atlantic Coastal Plain.

directions: Plant in moist to well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun in woodland garden settings; buckthorn bumelia is a native thorny shrub of the eastern Gulf and Atlantic Coastal Plain, growing naturally in moist, mixed woodlands and forest edges from Virginia to Texas. Unlike the more drought-adapted gum bumelia, this species prefers moist woodland soils and grows in shade-adapted situations at woodland margins and in the forest understory. The small, black fruits in fall are eaten by numerous species of birds and the thorny branches provide valuable nesting habitat. A useful shrub for creating dense, bird-friendly hedgerows at the edge of native woodland garden designs in zones 6–9. The small white flowers in summer attract pollinators.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or container stock in spring in moist, humus-rich, strongly acidic, well-drained to moist soil in partial shade; woolly sweetspire is a Coastal Plain relative of the common summersweet (C. alnifolia) with notably woolly-hairy leaves and stems.

directions: Plant in moist to wet, strongly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade; woolly sweetspire is a native ericaceous shrub of wet, acidic Coastal Plain habitats — pocosins, bay edges, moist pine flatwoods, and the margins of Carolina bays — from Virginia to Georgia. The dense, white, fragrant flower racemes in midsummer are intensely perfumed and highly attractive to native bees, particularly bumblebees, which find the tubular flowers accessible. The woolly-textured leaves and stems (more densely hairy than C. alnifolia) give the plant a distinctive soft appearance. Fall foliage turns orange to red. As with all sweetspires, it thrives in moist, acidic woodland gardens and wetland edges — excellent for rain gardens and moist shade borders in zones 5–9. Spreads slowly by stolons to form colonies.

planting.note: Direct sow fresh acorns in fall immediately after collection, covering 1–2 inches deep; Margaret's oak grows naturally in dry, sandy, infertile soils of scrub and sandhills habitats of the Gulf and Atlantic Coastal Plain. An important scrub oak adapted to xeric, nutrient-poor conditions.

directions: Plant in dry to very dry, sandy, infertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Margaret's oak is a scrub white oak complex species of the sandy longleaf pine ecosystem — growing in the xeric sandhills and scrub flatwoods of the Gulf and Atlantic Coastal Plain where it forms a key component of the dry longleaf pine savanna understory. The tree is smaller than the closely related post oak (Q. stellata) and more adapted to dry, sandy, low-nutrient conditions. The acorns are an important mast crop for deer, turkey, squirrels, and blue jays in the longleaf pine ecosystem. A valuable restoration species for degraded sandhills and scrub habitats, and an excellent drought-adapted native shade tree for dry, sandy landscapes in zone 9a where it survives with no supplemental water once established.

planting.note: Direct sow fresh acorns immediately after collection in fall, 1–2 inches deep in moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil; Nuttall oak is one of the most flood-tolerant large oaks, naturally growing in seasonally flooded bottomlands and swamp margins of the lower Mississippi River Valley.

directions: Plant in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral, deep alluvial soil in full sun; Nuttall oak is a large red oak complex member native to the bottomland hardwood forests and swamp margins of the lower Mississippi and Gulf Coastal Plain, from Missouri to Louisiana and east to Alabama. It is one of the most flood-tolerant large red oaks, surviving extended seasonal flooding that would kill most upland oaks. The acorns are produced in large quantities and are critically important for wood ducks, mallards, and other bottomland wildlife — among the most valuable waterfowl mast trees in the Mississippi Alluvial Valley. The tree grows rapidly compared to other oaks and is widely planted in bottomland reforestation and urban stormwater management projects. An outstanding large shade tree for moist to wet sites in zones 5–9 that combines ornamental value with exceptional ecological productivity.
